library.title: Top 50 Sandbag & Non-Traditional Strength Exercise Library

cards.name: Sandbag Bear-Hug Squat
cards.cardSummary: A front-loaded sandbag squat held tight against the chest to build leg strength, trunk bracing, and upright position control.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Output/power work and before late conditioning; use after athletes are warm enough to load safely.
cards.description: A front-loaded sandbag squat held tight against the chest to build leg strength, trunk bracing, and upright position control.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: squat_knee_dominant_strength. Strength is the main score. Explosiveness is secondary (2/5) only if coached with crisp intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move the load with control, stay braced, and finish every rep in the same shape you started.

scoring.explosiveDeliveryNote: Secondary only: use fast concentric intent, low reps/short distances, full rest, and stop before fatigue turns it into conditioning.

cards.whyItWorks: Bear-hug loading forces the athlete to create whole-body tension while squatting through a usable range without relying on a rigid bar path.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the exercise uses progressive loaded work to build strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve.
cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is turning the card into a race, finisher, or sloppy high-rep circuit. Keep load, reps, and rest aligned with strength, not exhaustion.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by load and range, then by tempo, leverage, distance, or support. Progress only when first and last reps look the same.

coachingExecution.movement_description: A front-loaded sandbag squat held tight against the chest to build leg strength, trunk bracing, and upright position control.
setup: Select a sandbag load the athlete can control without rushing or changing position.
Grip or hug the sandbag so it stays close to the body before the first rep.
Clear the training lane and confirm the athlete understands the start and finish position.
Set feet and stance width before loading; use a range the athlete can own.
execution_steps: Brace before descending.
Sit into the target range with knees tracking over toes.
Drive the floor away and stand tall without leaning or twisting.
coach_cues: Own the load before you move it.
Get tight before the object leaves the floor.
breathing_cues: Reset breath between reps or short carry lengths.
common_faults: Chasing speed or fatigue instead of force quality.
Losing brace before the hardest part of the rep.
Knees collapse inward.
Depth exceeds controlled range.
quality_gate: Athlete can explain and demonstrate the start and finish position.
Load path stays controlled without twisting or collapsing.
The last rep/step matches the first rep/step.
stop_signs: Pain, sharp discomfort, dizziness, or numbness.
Load cannot be controlled or set down safely.
Brace, posture, or alignment breaks for more than one rep/step.
Athlete rounds hard or twists unexpectedly while lifting from the floor.

dosage.notes: Strength dose: controlled eccentrics, crisp concentrics, and enough rest that reps do not become conditioning.

scaling.youth_beginner: Start with pattern rehearsal and a very light load. Use lighter load, shorter range, slower tempo, and fewer reps until positions are consistent. Keep sets short and stop before fatigue changes shape.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate load for low reps or short distance. Require identical first and last reps before adding load.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after demonstrating full control, no breath-holding panic, and stable trunk position under fatigue.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative loading and coach setup closely. Use lighter load, shorter range, slower tempo, and fewer reps until positions are consistent.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use heavier loading, longer rest, pauses, tempo, or slightly longer distances while preserving strength intent and technical quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, higher handles/elevated starts if needed, shorter ranges, and longer rest. Avoid maximal awkward loading unless highly trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance. Avoid breath-holding, maximal bracing, heavy front compression, or unstable transitions; substitute supported, lighter, or unloaded variants.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender split. Scale by training age, body size, strength history, joint comfort, and bracing strategy.
pairsWellBefore: Sustained Capacity only after full recovery
doNotUseWhen: Do not use as a conditioning race or burnout finisher.
Do not place before freshness-sensitive Output work if the load is heavy.
Do not load through pain, unstable joints, or uncontrolled spinal position.
mediaReferences: NSCA Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning - resistance training principles
Vortex exercise_card_details_for_llm.md - Card v2 authoring and Capacity phase logic
mediaInternalNotes: Keep as strength-first capacity card; do not auto-classify as Output unless delivery, dose, and rest are rewritten.
Explosiveness score is intentionally secondary and delivery-dependent.

readiness_checks: Athlete can demonstrate the unloaded pattern without pain.
Athlete can brace and breathe without losing posture.
Training area is clear and load can be bailed or set down safely.
contraindications: Current pain in the loaded joints or spine.
Inability to maintain neutral/braced trunk under load.
Uncontrolled hypertension or medical restriction against heavy bracing unless cleared.
common_substitutions: Sled or carry variation with shorter distance

setup: Select a sandbag load the athlete can control without rushing or changing position.

Stop before posture, grip, or breathing quality breaks.

cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is turning the card into a race, finisher, or sloppy high-rep circuit. Keep load, reps, and rest aligned with strength, not exhaustion.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by load and range, then by tempo, leverage, distance, or support. Progress only when first and last reps look the same.

coachingExecution.movement_description: A floor-based press with a sandbag that builds horizontal pressing strength and shoulder stability through an unstable load.
setup: Select a sandbag load the athlete can control without rushing or changing position.
Grip or hug the sandbag so it stays close to the body before the first rep.
Clear the training lane and confirm the athlete understands the start and finish position.
Stack wrists, elbows, and shoulders; brace before pressing.
execution_steps: Create whole-body tension before the press.
Press through a controlled path without shrugging or flaring early.
Lock out with ribs down and shoulders controlled.
coach_cues: Own the load before you move it.
Get tight before the object leaves the floor.
breathing_cues: Reset breath between reps or short carry lengths.
common_faults: Chasing speed or fatigue instead of force quality.
Losing brace before the hardest part of the rep.
Rib flare or low-back arch during the press.
Shoulders dump forward at lockout.
quality_gate: Athlete can explain and demonstrate the start and finish position.
Load path stays controlled without twisting or collapsing.
The last rep/step matches the first rep/step.
stop_signs: Pain, sharp discomfort, dizziness, or numbness.
Load cannot be controlled or set down safely.
Brace, posture, or alignment breaks for more than one rep/step.
Athlete rounds hard or twists unexpectedly while lifting from the floor.

dosage.notes: Strength dose: controlled eccentrics, crisp concentrics, and enough rest that reps do not become conditioning.

scaling.youth_beginner: Start with pattern rehearsal and a very light load. Use lighter load, shorter range, slower tempo, and fewer reps until positions are consistent. Keep sets short and stop before fatigue changes shape.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate load for low reps or short distance. Require identical first and last reps before adding load.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after demonstrating full control, no breath-holding panic, and stable trunk position under fatigue.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative loading and coach setup closely. Use lighter load, shorter range, slower tempo, and fewer reps until positions are consistent.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use heavier loading, longer rest, pauses, tempo, or slightly longer distances while preserving strength intent and technical quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, higher handles/elevated starts if needed, shorter ranges, and longer rest. Avoid maximal awkward loading unless highly trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance. Avoid breath-holding, maximal bracing, heavy front compression, or unstable transitions; substitute supported, lighter, or unloaded variants.

cards.name: Rope Climb with Foot Lock
cards.cardSummary: A controlled rope climb using a foot lock to build grip, pulling strength, trunk tension, and coordinated climbing positions.
cards.bestPlacement: Capacity phase after Output/power work and before late conditioning; use after athletes are warm enough to load safely.
cards.description: A controlled rope climb using a foot lock to build grip, pulling strength, trunk tension, and coordinated climbing positions.
cards.coachLanguage: Primary subrole: pull_hang_grip_strength. Strength is the main score. Explosiveness is secondary (1/5) only if coached with crisp intent, low fatigue, and adequate rest.
cards.athleteLanguage: Move the load with control, stay braced, and finish every rep in the same shape you started.

scoring.explosiveDeliveryNote: Secondary only: use fast concentric intent, low reps/short distances, full rest, and stop before fatigue turns it into conditioning.

cards.whyItWorks: The foot lock lets athletes train meaningful rope strength while scaling arm demand and safety.
cards.whyItGoesHere: Belongs in Capacity because the exercise uses progressive loaded work to build strength, force production, tissue tolerance, and structural reserve.
cards.commonMisuse: The main mistake is turning the card into a race, finisher, or sloppy high-rep circuit. Keep load, reps, and rest aligned with strength, not exhaustion.
cards.scalingGuidance: Scale first by load and range, then by tempo, leverage, distance, or support. Progress only when first and last reps look the same.

coachingExecution.movement_description: A controlled rope climb using a foot lock to build grip, pulling strength, trunk tension, and coordinated climbing positions.
setup: Select a rope load the athlete can control without rushing or changing position.
Check rope security and give enough space for pulling or climbing without crowding.
Clear the training lane and confirm the athlete understands the start and finish position.
Set shoulders active and avoid passive hanging or rounded pulling posture.
execution_steps: Start each pull from a stable brace.
Pull with elbows and shoulder blades together, not the neck.
Control the return so tension stays in the target tissues.
Start from active shoulders.
Use strict pulling or climbing mechanics without kicking for momentum.
coach_cues: Own the load before you move it.
breathing_cues: Reset breath between reps or short carry lengths.
common_faults: Chasing speed or fatigue instead of force quality.
Losing brace before the hardest part of the rep.
Shrugging into the neck.
Using body swing instead of controlled pulling.
quality_gate: Athlete can explain and demonstrate the start and finish position.
Load path stays controlled without twisting or collapsing.
The last rep/step matches the first rep/step.
stop_signs: Pain, sharp discomfort, dizziness, or numbness.
Load cannot be controlled or set down safely.
Brace, posture, or alignment breaks for more than one rep/step.
Grip or shoulder control fails while suspended.

dosage.notes: Use strict reps with full control; add load or leverage only after repeatable clean reps.

scaling.youth_beginner: Start with pattern rehearsal and a very light load. Use lower bodyweight demand, feet assistance, lower height, or partial range until shoulders and grip stay controlled. Keep sets short and stop before fatigue changes shape.
scaling.youth_intermediate: Use moderate load for low reps or short distance. Require identical first and last reps before adding load.
scaling.teen: Progress load gradually after demonstrating full control, no breath-holding panic, and stable trunk position under fatigue.
scaling.adult_beginner: Use conservative loading and coach setup closely. Use lower bodyweight demand, feet assistance, lower height, or partial range until shoulders and grip stay controlled.
scaling.adult_advanced: Use heavier loading, longer rest, pauses, tempo, or slightly longer distances while preserving strength intent and technical quality.
scaling.older_adult: Use lighter load, higher handles/elevated starts if needed, shorter ranges, and longer rest. Avoid maximal awkward loading unless highly trained.
scaling.pregnancy_postpartum: Use only with medical clearance. Avoid breath-holding, maximal bracing, heavy front compression, or unstable transitions; substitute supported, lighter, or unloaded variants.

cards.genderSpecificNotes: No default gender split. Scale by training age, body size, strength history, joint comfort, and bracing strategy.
